epigraph.text:
The heavens declare the glory of God; the skies 
proclaim the work of his hands.

content.text:
When mathematicians write $x + 5$, they are not being mysterious. 
The letter $x$ simply represents a number we don't know yet—or a 
number that could take many different values. This simple idea 
transforms arithmetic into algebra.

content.title: Variable
content.content:
A **variable** is a letter or symbol that represents 
a number. Common variable letters include $x$, $y$, $z$, $a$, $b$, $c$, $n$, and $t$.

items: Write general rules that work for many numbers

content.title: Variables in context
content.problem:
Identify the variable and explain what it represents:

1. The perimeter of a square is $P = 4s$

2. A taxi charges $5 + 2m$ dollars for a ride of $m$ miles

3. Your age in $t$ years will be $14 + t$
content.solution:
1. Variable $s$ represents the side length; $P$ represents perimeter

2. Variable $m$ represents the number of miles traveled

3. Variable $t$ represents the number of years from now

content.text:
Ren\'{e Descartes}{Each problem that I solved became a rule, 
which served afterwards to solve other problems.}

content.title: Algebraic Expression
content.content:
An **algebraic expression** is a 
mathematical phrase that contains numbers, variables, and operation 
symbols ($+$, $-$, $\times$, $\div$).

content.text:
Note: An algebraic expression does *not* contain an equals sign. 
When an equals sign is present, we have an *equation*, not just 
an expression.

content.title: Terms, Coefficients, and Constants
content.content:
- A **term** is a part of an expression separated 
 by addition or subtraction signs

- A **coefficient** is the numerical factor 
 of a term containing a variable

- A **constant** is a term with no variable

content.title: Identifying parts
content.problem:
For the expression $5x^2 - 3x + 7$, identify:

1. The terms

2. The coefficients

3. The constant

content.text:
In algebra, we usually omit the multiplication sign to avoid confusion 
with the variable $x$. These all mean the same thing:

content.title: Writing Multiplication in Algebra
content.content:
- Write the coefficient (number) first: $3x$ not $x3$

- Write variables in alphabetical order: $2ab$ not $2ba$

- $1 \cdot x$ is written simply as $x$ (coefficient of 1 is implied)

- $(-1) \cdot x$ is written as $-x$

content.title: Rewriting expressions
content.problem:
Rewrite using standard algebraic notation:

1. $4 \times y$

2. $n \times 7$

3. $1 \times a + b \times (-3)$

4. $x \times x \times x$

content.text: When a variable is multiplied by itself, we use exponents:

$$x \cdot x &= x^2 \quad \text{(``$x$ squared'')} \\
x \cdot x \cdot x &= x^3 \quad \text{(``$x$ cubed'')} \\
x \cdot x \cdot x \cdot x &= x^4 \quad \text{(``$x$ to the fourth'')}$$

content.title: Working with exponents
content.problem:
Write in exponential form:

1. $y \cdot y \cdot y \cdot y \cdot y$

2. $3 \cdot a \cdot a \cdot b \cdot b \cdot b$

content.title: The Origin of $x$
content.content:
Why do we use $x$ for the unknown? Some historians believe it came from 
the Arabic word *shay* (thing), which was written *xay* in 
medieval Spanish. Ren\'{e} Descartes popularized using letters from the 
end of the alphabet ($x$, $y$, $z$) for unknowns and letters from the 
beginning ($a$, $b$, $c$) for known quantities in his 1637 work 
*La G\'{e*om\'{e}trie}.

marginNotes.text:
For now we see only a reflection as 
in a mirror; then we shall see face to face.

Just as a body, though one, has 
many parts, but all its many parts form one body, so it is with Christ.

I will surely bless you and make your 
descendants as numerous as the stars in the sky.

marginNotes.author: Carl Friedrich Gauss
marginNotes.text:
It is not knowledge, but the act of 
learning, that grants the greatest enjoyment.
